findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, fusiform soft tissue mass located within the post-styloid carotid space. The mass classically displaces the internal carotid artery anteriorly and the internal jugular vein posterolaterally.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include a 'target sign' appearance on T2-weighted MRI, consisting of a hyperintense myxoid periphery and a hypointense fibrocollagenous centre. Furthermore, a 'split-fat' sign may be observed as the mass displaces surrounding normal fat along the path of the exiting nerve.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of splaying of the internal and external carotid arteries to suggest a carotid body tumour. Furthermore, there are no prominent serpentine flow voids giving a 'salt and pepper' appearance to suggest a highly vascular paraganglioma.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the fusiform shape of the mass displacing the internal carotid artery anteriorly without prominent flow voids, combined with a T2 'target sign'.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Nerve sheath tumor (vagus schwannoma), given the fusiform shape, T2 target sign, and specific displacement pattern within the carotid space. Schwannomas frequently arise along the course of the vagus nerve and characteristically separate the internal carotid artery and internal jugular vein.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Paraganglioma, however these typically demonstrate a highly vascular 'salt and pepper' appearance on MRI due to numerous flow voids and microhaemorrhages. Furthermore, a carotid body paraganglioma classically splays the internal and external carotid arteries rather than displacing the internal carotid artery anteriorly.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Vascular abnormality, given that a partially thrombosed pseudoaneurysm or internal carotid artery dissection can mimic an enhancing mass, and missing this could lead to catastrophic haemorrhage if a biopsy is inadvertently attempted. However, the presence of intact flow within the nondisplaced arterial lumen on MRI helps rule this out.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to meticulously define the anatomical relationship of the mass to the major vessels and adjacent cranial nerves to guide surgical planning.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to confirm the typically slow growth rate of a schwannoma and to identify any previously overlooked lesions that might suggest a syndromic association such as neurofibromatosis type 2.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the neck, as this modality is superior here for characterizing the internal tissue architecture, identifying the 'target' and 'split-fat' signs, and accurately mapping the extent of the lesion.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess for the characteristic intense enhancement, alongside T2-weighted sequences to look for the hyperintense myxoid periphery and to confidently exclude the flow voids typical of a paraganglioma.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Head and Neck MDT. My report would clearly document the precise dimensions of the mass, the exact displacement of the internal carotid artery and internal jugular vein, and any extension towards the skull base or jugular foramen.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, fusiform soft tissue mass located within the post-styloid carotid space. The mass classically displaces the internal carotid artery anteriorly and the internal jugular vein posterolaterally. Supporting features include a 'target sign' appearance on T2-weighted MRI, consisting of a hyperintense myxoid periphery and a hypointense fibrocollagenous centre. Furthermore, a 'split-fat' sign may be observed as the mass displaces surrounding normal fat along the path of the exiting nerve. Importantly, there is no evidence of splaying of the internal and external carotid arteries to suggest a carotid body tumour. Furthermore, there are no prominent serpentine flow voids giving a 'salt and pepper' appearance to suggest a highly vascular paraganglioma. The feature that most influences my thinking is the fusiform shape of the mass displacing the internal carotid artery anteriorly without prominent flow voids, combined with a T2 'target sign'.

The appearances are most in keeping with Nerve sheath tumor (vagus schwannoma), given the fusiform shape, T2 target sign, and specific displacement pattern within the carotid space. Schwannomas frequently arise along the course of the vagus nerve and characteristically separate the internal carotid artery and internal jugular vein. The principle differential would be Paraganglioma, however these typically demonstrate a highly vascular 'salt and pepper' appearance on MRI due to numerous flow voids and microhaemorrhages. Furthermore, a carotid body paraganglioma classically splays the internal and external carotid arteries rather than displacing the internal carotid artery anteriorly. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Vascular abnormality, given that a partially thrombosed pseudoaneurysm or internal carotid artery dissection can mimic an enhancing mass, and missing this could lead to catastrophic haemorrhage if a biopsy is inadvertently attempted. However, the presence of intact flow within the nondisplaced arterial lumen on MRI helps rule this out.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to meticulously define the anatomical relationship of the mass to the major vessels and adjacent cranial nerves to guide surgical planning. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to confirm the typically slow growth rate of a schwannoma and to identify any previously overlooked lesions that might suggest a syndromic association such as neurofibromatosis type 2. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the neck, as this modality is superior here for characterizing the internal tissue architecture, identifying the 'target' and 'split-fat' signs, and accurately mapping the extent of the lesion. I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess for the characteristic intense enhancement, alongside T2-weighted sequences to look for the hyperintense myxoid periphery and to confidently exclude the flow voids typical of a paraganglioma. This should go to the Head and Neck MDT. My report would clearly document the precise dimensions of the mass, the exact displacement of the internal carotid artery and internal jugular vein, and any extension towards the skull base or jugular foramen.
